Questions.Title: Purpose of statistical inference
Questions.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of statistical inference?
Questions.Hint: Think about what we do when we cannot examine every individual in a population.
Questions.Feedback: Statistical inference allows researchers to draw conclusions about a population based on sample data, rather than examining every individual observation.

Options.Text: To draw conclusions about a population based on sample data
To collect data from every member of a population
To eliminate all uncertainty from data analysis
To describe only the sample without making broader claims

Questions.Title: Null hypothesis definition
Questions.QuestionText: Which of the following best describes the null hypothesis (H₀) in a hypothesis test?
Questions.Hint: Consider which hypothesis represents the 'no effect' or 'no difference' position.
Questions.Feedback: The null hypothesis is the default statement that assumes no effect, no difference, or no relationship. It is the claim the researcher seeks to find evidence against.

Options.Text: A statement asserting no effect or no difference between groups
A statement that confirms the researcher's expected finding
The hypothesis that is always accepted after data collection
A statement that quantifies the size of the observed effect

Questions.Title: Role of a test statistic
Questions.QuestionText: What does a test statistic measure in a hypothesis test?
Questions.Hint: Consider how far the observed data are from what was expected under H₀.
Questions.Feedback: A test statistic is a numerical value calculated from sample data that summarizes how far the observed result is from what would be expected if the null hypothesis were true.

Options.Text: How far the observed result is from what is expected under the null hypothesis
The probability that the null hypothesis is correct
The size of the population from which data were sampled
The confidence level chosen by the researcher

Questions.Title: Type I error definition
Questions.QuestionText: A Type I error in hypothesis testing is best described as which of the following?
Questions.Hint: Think about a 'false positive' — concluding there is an effect when there really is none.
Questions.Feedback: A Type I error occurs when the null hypothesis is true but is incorrectly rejected, producing a false positive conclusion. Its probability is controlled by the significance level alpha.

Options.Text: Rejecting a true null hypothesis, resulting in a false positive
Failing to reject a false null hypothesis, resulting in a false negative
Selecting an inappropriate test statistic for the data
Collecting an insufficient sample to detect an effect

Questions.Title: Meaning of a p-value
Questions.QuestionText: What does a p-value of 0.03 mean in the context of hypothesis testing?
Questions.Hint: Recall the definition of a p-value: a probability calculated assuming H₀ is true.
Questions.Feedback: A p-value of 0.03 means there is a 3% probability of observing data at least as extreme as those obtained, assuming the null hypothesis is true. It does not mean there is a 3% chance that H₀ is true.

Options.Text: There is a 3% chance of observing data this extreme if H₀ is true
There is a 3% chance that the null hypothesis is true
The effect size is 3% larger in the treatment group
The researcher has a 97% chance of being correct

Questions.Title: Alpha level selection timing
Questions.QuestionText: When should the significance threshold (alpha level) be chosen in a hypothesis test?
Questions.Hint: Think about when choices should be made to avoid bias introduced by seeing the data first.
Questions.Feedback: The significance threshold should be chosen before data collection begins. Choosing alpha after seeing the data is a source of bias known as p-hacking and invalidates the test's integrity.

Options.Text: Before data collection, based on the research context and consequences of errors
After data collection, once the p-value has been computed
After the alternative hypothesis has been confirmed
Only when the sample size exceeds 1,000 observations

Questions.Title: Reducing Type II errors
Questions.QuestionText: Which of the following is one of the most effective ways to reduce the probability of a Type II error?
Questions.Hint: Consider what gives a test more power to detect a real effect.
Questions.Feedback: Increasing sample size is one of the most effective ways to reduce Type II errors because larger samples provide more information, increasing the statistical power (1 − β) of the test.

Options.Text: Increasing the sample size
Lowering the alpha level
Using a one-tailed instead of a two-tailed test structure
Reporting only statistically significant results

Questions.Title: Best practice p-value reporting
Questions.QuestionText: According to best practices, what should accompany a p-value when reporting statistical significance?
Questions.Hint: Think about what additional information conveys the direction and magnitude of an effect.
Questions.Feedback: Best practice requires reporting effect size measures (such as Cohen's d or R²) and confidence intervals alongside p-values to convey both the direction and magnitude of an effect, not just whether it crossed a significance threshold.

Options.Text: An effect size measure and a confidence interval
Only the sample size used in the analysis
The raw data values for every observation
A second p-value calculated with a different alpha level

Questions.Title: A/B testing control and treatment
Questions.QuestionText: In an A/B test, what do the labels 'A' and 'B' represent?
Questions.Hint: Think about the two versions being compared in a controlled business experiment.
Questions.Feedback: In an A/B test, A represents the control (the existing version) and B represents the treatment (the new or modified version). The goal is to determine which version performs better on a defined metric.

Options.Text: A is the control version and B is the treatment version
A is the alternative hypothesis and B is the null hypothesis
A is the larger sample group and B is the smaller sample group
A is the post-test result and B is the pre-test result

Questions.Title: Peeking at A/B test results
Questions.QuestionText: Why is 'peeking' at A/B test results before the planned end date considered a methodological error?
Questions.Hint: Consider what peeking does to the fixed-sample test framework and error rates.
Questions.Feedback: Peeking at results before the planned end date and potentially stopping early inflates the false-positive rate because the fixed-sample hypothesis test framework assumes data collection is complete before the test is evaluated.

Options.Text: It inflates false-positive rates by invalidating the fixed-sample test framework
It reduces the sample size below the minimum required threshold
It changes the null hypothesis mid-experiment
It forces researchers to use non-parametric tests instead of t-tests

Questions.Title: Effect size in A/B testing
Questions.QuestionText: Why is effect size important in A/B testing decisions even when a result is statistically significant?
Questions.Hint: Think about a situation where a tiny difference is detected only because the sample is very large.
Questions.Feedback: Effect size quantifies the magnitude of the difference between groups. A statistically significant result with a very small effect size (e.g., a 0.1% lift in conversion) may be financially negligible, so effect size helps bridge the gap between statistical and practical significance.

Options.Text: It reveals whether the statistically significant difference is large enough to matter in practice
It determines whether the null hypothesis should be accepted
It replaces the need to calculate a p-value
It confirms that the experimental design was properly randomized

Questions.Title: Pearson vs. Spearman correlation
Questions.QuestionText: When should the Spearman rank correlation coefficient be preferred over the Pearson correlation coefficient?
Questions.Hint: Think about what types of data or distributions make Pearson's r less appropriate.
Questions.Feedback: Spearman's ρ is preferred when data contain outliers, are not normally distributed, or are measured on an ordinal scale, because it is based on ranked order rather than raw values and is therefore non-parametric.

Options.Text: When data contain outliers or are measured on an ordinal scale
When both variables are continuous and normally distributed
When the researcher wants to establish causation between variables
When the sample size is larger than 1,000 observations

Questions.Title: Correlation coefficient range
Questions.QuestionText: What is the range of possible values for a correlation coefficient?
Questions.Hint: Think about the extreme values that indicate perfect positive and perfect negative relationships.
Questions.Feedback: Correlation coefficients range from -1 to +1. A value of -1 indicates a perfect negative relationship, +1 indicates a perfect positive relationship, and 0 indicates no linear relationship.

Options.Text: 0 to 1
0 to infinity

Questions.Title: Correlation versus causation
Questions.QuestionText: Which of the following best illustrates why correlation does not imply causation?
Questions.Hint: Think about two variables that both increase due to a third underlying factor rather than influencing each other directly.
Questions.Feedback: The classic example is the correlation between ice cream sales and drowning rates — both rise in summer, but ice cream does not cause drowning. The confounding variable is hot weather. This illustrates that a shared cause can produce correlation without direct causation.

Options.Text: Ice cream sales and drowning rates both rise in summer due to hot weather, not because one causes the other
Hours studied and exam scores are positively correlated, proving studying causes higher grades
A high R² in regression proves the predictor variable causes changes in the outcome
A p-value below 0.05 confirms a causal relationship between two variables

Questions.Title: OLS regression coefficient estimation
Questions.QuestionText: What criterion does Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) use to estimate regression coefficients?
Questions.Hint: Think about what OLS minimizes when fitting a line to data.
Questions.Feedback: OLS estimates regression coefficients by minimizing the sum of squared differences between the observed values and the values predicted by the model, producing the best linear unbiased estimates (BLUE) under standard assumptions.

Options.Text: Minimizing the sum of squared differences between observed and predicted values
Maximizing the R² value of the fitted model
Minimizing the absolute value of the correlation coefficient
Maximizing the number of statistically significant predictors in the model

Questions.Title: Interpreting R-squared in regression
Questions.QuestionText: If a simple linear regression model has an R² of 0.85, what does this mean?
Questions.Hint: R² expresses explained variance as a proportion of total variance in the outcome variable.
Questions.Feedback: An R² of 0.85 means that 85% of the variance in the outcome variable Y is explained by the predictor variable X in the regression model. However, a high R² alone does not guarantee the model is appropriate.

Options.Text: 85% of the variance in the outcome variable is explained by the predictor
The predictor variable causes 85% of the changes in the outcome
The model correctly predicts 85% of individual observations exactly
There is an 85% probability that the regression slope is statistically significant

Questions.Title: Multicollinearity detection tool
Questions.QuestionText: Which diagnostic tool is the standard method for detecting multicollinearity in multiple regression?
Questions.Hint: Think about the statistic calculated for each predictor that measures how much its variance is inflated by correlation with other predictors.
Questions.Feedback: The Variance Inflation Factor (VIF) is the standard diagnostic tool for detecting multicollinearity. A VIF above 5 or 10 is commonly used as a threshold indicating problematic multicollinearity.

Options.Text: Variance Inflation Factor (VIF)
Breusch-Pagan test
Shapiro-Wilk test
Adjusted R²

Questions.Title: Adjusted R² versus R²
Questions.QuestionText: Why is Adjusted R² preferred over R² when comparing multiple regression models with different numbers of predictors?
Questions.Hint: Consider what happens to R² every time a new predictor is added to the model, regardless of whether it is useful.
Questions.Feedback: R² always increases as predictors are added, even if those predictors add no real explanatory value. Adjusted R² penalizes for the number of predictors included, making it a more appropriate measure for comparing models with different numbers of predictors.

Options.Text: Because R² always increases when predictors are added, regardless of their usefulness
Because Adjusted R² can exceed 1.0 for very well-fitting models
Because R² cannot be calculated for models with more than two predictors
Because Adjusted R² measures statistical significance directly

Questions.Title: Selecting the correct statistical test
Questions.QuestionText: According to the module, which statistical method should be used when the goal is to predict a continuous outcome from one or more predictor variables?
Questions.Hint: Think about the method that explicitly models the relationship between predictors and an outcome variable.
Questions.Feedback: Regression analysis is the appropriate method when predicting a continuous outcome from one or more predictors. Correlation assesses relationships, and t-tests compare means between groups, but regression is designed for prediction and modeling.

Options.Text: Regression analysis
Chi-square test
Pearson correlation only
Independent samples t-test

Questions.Title: Communicating results to stakeholders
Questions.QuestionText: Which of the following tools is recommended to help non-technical stakeholders understand complex statistical relationships?
Questions.Hint: Think about visual representations mentioned in the module that make results accessible to broad audiences.
Questions.Feedback: The module recommends visualization tools such as scatter plots with regression lines, residual plots, and annotated bar charts to help non-technical stakeholders grasp complex statistical relationships quickly and clearly.

Options.Text: Scatter plots with regression lines, residual plots, and annotated bar charts
Raw data tables listing every individual observation
Mathematical derivations of the OLS estimator
Detailed descriptions of the null and alternative hypotheses only
